questions.scenario: Two operators ran 'ls /home/student/logs' and 'ls /home/student/data' on different machines. You need to compare the outputs without creating temp files.
questions.question: Use process substitution to diff the output of 'ls /home/student/logs' against 'ls /home/student/data'.
questions.description: Process substitution <(cmd) runs a command and presents its output as a virtual file. diff needs two filenames — process substitution lets you pass command output directly, no temp files needed.
use_cases: Compare outputs of two commands without temp files
Diff two remote file listings over SSH
Compare sorted vs unsorted versions of the same data
hints: diff takes two filenames; use <(cmd) as each one

questions.scenario: You want to sort the lines of /home/student/data/users.csv and diff it against the original to see which lines are out of order.
questions.question: Diff the original /home/student/data/users.csv against a sorted version of itself using process substitution.
questions.description: <(cmd) treats command output as a file descriptor. You can use the same source file inside a <() and do real-time transformation. This avoids any temp files or intermediate writes.
use_cases: Verify a file is already sorted
Compare raw vs processed versions of data
Find lines that change under transformation
hints: diff takes two arguments — use <() for the sorted version
sort reads the file, <() wraps it

questions.scenario: You need to count how many lines are in the output of 'ls /home/student' without storing it anywhere.
questions.question: Use process substitution to count the lines output by 'ls /home/student' using wc.
questions.description: wc expects a filename or stdin. <(cmd) gives wc a virtual filename backed by the command's output. Alternative to ls /home/student | wc -l, but useful when you need to pass the output to a command that only accepts filenames.
use_cases: Count lines in a command's output when piping isn't possible
Pass command output to tools that only accept file arguments
Measure output size without temp files
hints: wc -l can take a filename or stdin

questions.scenario: You want to log the output of a long-running command to a file via process substitution while still reading it in the pipeline.
questions.question: Use >(tee /tmp/ps-log.txt) to log 'echo reactor status ok' output while also passing it through cat.
questions.description: >(cmd) is output process substitution — it creates a virtual file you can write to, and writes are piped into cmd. Used to fan out writes: send to multiple destinations simultaneously.
use_cases: Log pipeline data to a file while continuing the pipeline
Send output to multiple sinks simultaneously
Transparently insert logging into an existing pipeline
hints: The > before ( makes it an output substitution
